attractions.name: Uluwatu Temple
attractions.description: A dramatic sea temple perched on a cliff edge on Bali's southern tip, famous for sunset views and evening Kecak fire dance performances.
attractions.transport_tips: Best reached by car/scooter from Seminyak or Kuta (~45-60 min)

attractions.name: Tanah Lot Temple
attractions.description: One of Bali's most photographed temples, sitting on a rock formation just offshore, especially striking at low tide and sunset.

attractions.name: Tegallalang Rice Terraces
attractions.description: Iconic stepped emerald-green rice paddies near Ubud, using the traditional Balinese 'subak' irrigation system.

attractions.name: Sacred Monkey Forest Sanctuary, Ubud
attractions.description: A forested sanctuary and temple complex in central Ubud, home to hundreds of long-tailed macaques roaming freely among ancient stone temples.

attractions.name: Tirta Empul Temple
attractions.description: A holy spring water temple near Tampaksiring where Balinese Hindus and visitors take part in a ritual purification bathing ceremony.

attractions.name: Mount Batur Sunrise Trek
attractions.description: A pre-dawn guided hike up an active volcano to watch sunrise over Lake Batur and neighbouring Mount Agung.
attractions.transport_tips: Guided tours include hotel pickup from Ubud

attractions.name: Nusa Penida Day Trip
attractions.description: A rugged island a fast boat ride from Bali, famous for the Kelingking Beach cliff viewpoint, Angel's Billabong, and Broken Beach.
attractions.transport_tips: Fast boats depart from Sanur; book a guided island tour as roads are rough

attractions.name: Seminyak & Kuta Beaches
attractions.description: Bali's most developed beach strip, popular for surfing lessons, beach clubs, and dramatic sunsets.
attractions.transport_tips: Well connected by taxi/scooter across South Bali

restaurants.name: Locavore, Ubud
restaurants.description: An internationally acclaimed fine-dining restaurant championing modern Indonesian cuisine using hyper-local ingredients — book weeks ahead.

restaurants.name: Warung Babi Guling Ibu Oka, Ubud
restaurants.description: A famous local warung serving Bali's signature suckling pig dish, babi guling, in a simple, authentic setting.

restaurants.name: La Brisa, Canggu
restaurants.description: A boat-shaped beach club and restaurant in Canggu known for sunset views, cocktails, and a laid-back beach vibe.

hidden_gems.name: Sidemen Valley
hidden_gems.description: A quiet valley of rice terraces and mountain views east of Ubud, far less touristy than Tegallalang, popular for slow travel and gentle walks.

hidden_gems.name: Gitgit Waterfall, North Bali
hidden_gems.description: A tall jungle waterfall near Singaraja, considerably quieter than the waterfalls closer to Ubud.

nightlife.name: Canggu Beach Clubs
nightlife.description: Canggu's beachfront clubs (Old Man's, Finns) combine surf culture with sunset DJ sets and a young, international crowd.

nightlife.name: Sky Garden, Kuta
nightlife.description: A multi-level nightclub complex in Kuta, one of Bali's longest-running large-scale party venues.

shopping.name: Ubud Art Market
shopping.description: A bustling market for Balinese handicrafts, sarongs, wood carvings, and souvenirs — bargaining is expected.

shopping.name: Seminyak Boutiques
shopping.description: Seminyak's Jalan Kayu Aya (Eat Street) area is lined with designer boutiques, homeware stores, and concept shops.

festivals.name: Nyepi (Day of Silence)
festivals.description: Bali's Hindu New Year, observed as a full day of island-wide silence — no travel, lights, or activity outdoors, preceded by vibrant Ogoh-ogoh effigy parades the night before.
festivals.best_time: March (dates follow the Balinese Saka calendar)

festivals.name: Galungan & Kuningan
festivals.description: A major Balinese Hindu holiday celebrating the triumph of good over evil, marked with tall bamboo penjor decorations lining the streets.
festivals.best_time: Occurs every 210 days on the Balinese pawukon calendar

adventure_activities.name: Surfing Lessons, Canggu/Kuta
adventure_activities.description: Beginner-friendly surf breaks with numerous surf schools offering lessons along Canggu and Kuta beaches.

adventure_activities.name: White-water Rafting, Ayung River
adventure_activities.description: Rapids rafting through jungle scenery on the Ayung river near Ubud, suitable for beginners.

local_tips: Rent a scooter for flexibility, but Bali traffic can be chaotic — an international driving permit is technically required and increasingly enforced.
Dress with a sarong and sash when entering temples; many temples provide rentals at the entrance.
South Bali (Seminyak, Canggu, Kuta) is beach/nightlife-focused; Ubud is cultural and jungle-focused — many itineraries split time between both.
Bali observes Nyepi (Day of Silence) once a year, during which the entire island, including the airport, shuts down for 24 hours — check dates if travelling in March.

safety.general: Bali is generally safe for tourists, but scooter accidents are the most common cause of tourist injury — wear a helmet and drive cautiously.
safety.water_safety: Ocean currents at popular surf beaches can be strong; swim at flagged, patrolled areas where available.
safety.belly_safety: Drink bottled or filtered water and be cautious with street-food ice to avoid stomach upsets.

culture.overview: Bali is a Hindu-majority island within Muslim-majority Indonesia, with daily life woven through temple ceremonies, offerings (canang sari), and a strong communal 'banjar' social structure.
culture.etiquette: Dress modestly at temples (sarong required), never step over offerings placed on the ground, and use your right hand for giving/receiving.

transport.getting_around: Scooters and app-based ride-hailing (Gojek/Grab) are the most flexible options; traffic in South Bali can be heavy, especially Canggu-Seminyak-Kuta.
transport.airport: Ngurah Rai International Airport (DPS) near Kuta serves the whole island.

visa_information.indian_passport_holders: Indonesia offers a Visa on Arrival (VOA) for Indian citizens, payable on entry or in advance online (e-VOA), valid for a set short-stay period and extendable once.
visa_information.general: Many nationalities are eligible for visa-free or visa-on-arrival entry to Indonesia for short tourist stays — check current rules before travel.

weather.avoid: January-February (peak wet season with heavy rain and rougher seas)
